narrative:
Pinebrook Advisory Group is a small management consultancy serving
mid-market operating companies, five people at the start of the window and
six by the end. The firm takes on a handful of engagements a year:
operational reviews, pricing studies, and post-acquisition integration
support. Its documents are lean and practical: engagement letters on firm
letterhead, kickoff memos, meeting minutes that name every attendee, and
status reports written for a client executive who skims. The Managing
Partner signs everything that leaves the building. Tone is plain American
business English, first names inside the firm, full names and titles when a
client is on the page.
instructions:
Author every document briefed in `docs`, as JSON matching the deliverable
schema:

  {"schema_id": "orgsmith/authoring-deliverable@1",
   "work_order_id": "wo:author:0004",
   "docs": [{"schema_id": "orgsmith/docir@1", "doc_id": "d:....",
             "blocks": [...]}, ...]}

Block kinds: heading (text, level), paragraph (text), list (items),
table (header, rows), sigblock (signers = person ids from the brief).

Hard rules:
- One DocIR per brief; doc_ids must match the work order exactly.
- Every fact id briefed for a doc MUST appear as a literal placeholder
  {{fact:<id>}} at least once in that doc's text, where <id> is the
  briefed id verbatim including its `f:` prefix (e.g.
  {{fact:f:E-2019-001.start}}). You do not know fact values; never write
  a number, date, or name in place of a placeholder.
- Use only placeholders for fact ids briefed on that document.
- Do not invent people, organizations, addresses, amounts, or dates.
  People and their titles come from the briefs; the org narrative sets
  tone only.
- Every entry in a doc's `mentions` list must appear VERBATIM (the exact
  `surface` string) somewhere in that doc's text. Sigblock signers count
  as mentions of themselves. Short surfaces are nicknames: work them into
  prose naturally ("...as {{nickname}} noted..."), alongside, not instead
  of, the person's full name.
- If a doc carries `engagement_position`, honor it: it states where the
  document sits in its engagement's timeline. Do not describe progress or a
  phase inconsistent with it, and do not state the engagement's start or
  end date (those are fact placeholders, not yours to write).
- If a doc carries `firm_digest`, it is the ONLY firm history you may treat
  as established as of this document's date. Reference clients only through
  the placeholders it names; claim no engagement it does not list; present
  nothing later than this document as already true.
- If a doc carries `reporting_line`, the person reports to exactly who it
  names and to no one else. Do not name any other person or title as their
  supervisor; a reporting relationship is owned by the ledger, not invented.
- Write plain, era-appropriate business prose in the org's voice.

docs.title: Engagement Letter: Integration Support for Reeves, Miller and Thompson

authors.name: Daniel Jones
authors.title: Managing Partner
authors.persona: Daniel reads every draft with a pen in his hand and a client executive in mind, and what he mostly does with the pen is cross things out. He wants the recommendation in the first paragraph and the reasoning underneath it, not the other way around, and he has no patience for hedging that exists only to protect the writer. In a room he is warm and unhurried, good at letting a client talk himself into the answer; on paper he is short. Because his signature goes on anything that leaves the firm, he treats the letterhead as a promise and reviews accordingly.

participants.name: Cynthia Ball
participants.title: Analyst
participants.persona: Cynthia is the analyst clients open up to. She has an easy way of asking about how the work actually gets done, and she comes back from a site visit with the detail nobody put in the data room. She turns messy interview notes into clean exhibits quickly and enjoys that part of the job more than the modeling. She is direct in meetings, occasionally more direct than the partners expect from an analyst, and she is rarely wrong when she pushes.

participants.name: Robert Miller
participants.title: Principal Consultant
participants.persona: Robert works operational reviews from the floor up. He would rather spend a morning walking a warehouse and talking to line supervisors than start from whatever the client's own reporting says, and he is quietly skeptical of any conclusion he cannot trace back to something he watched happen. His first drafts run long because he puts everything in, then he cuts hard on the second pass. With clients he is patient and a little dry, the sort who will let an uncomfortable silence sit until somebody fills it.

participants.name: Jennifer Fletcher
participants.title: Senior Consultant
participants.persona: Jennifer structures a problem fast, usually on a whiteboard, and she is comfortable telling a client early that the thing they asked for is not the thing they need. She carries the pricing work and most of the integration engagements, where the real job is getting two groups of people to agree on what a word means. Her memos read the way she talks, plain and slightly clipped, with the awkward finding put plainly instead of buried on the last page. She is generous with the analysts about method and blunt with them about sloppy exhibits.

docs.engagement_summary: Integration Support for Reeves, Miller and Thompson, running about 7 months. Commercial terms and dates are briefed as fact placeholders only.

facts.hint: amount of money; place where the figure belongs
calendar date; place where the date belongs
proper name; place where the name belongs

docs.guidance: Formal engagement letter on firm letterhead confirming the engagement. Do NOT repeat the firm's name as a heading or title line: the letterhead already carries it. Open with the client's inside address and a salutation, then cover scope and approach, the team, and the fee and start date, followed by the standard terms a real engagement letter carries -- each as its own short headed paragraph: fees and payment, term and termination, confidentiality, limitation of liability, and governing law. Close with a signature request and end with a sigblock block signed by the letter's author and the client contact. Your personal writing style, held consistently: a crisp register (short declarative sentences, few adjectives, no flourishes) in long sentences. You habitually: avoids lists entirely and writes in paragraphs; opens with the conclusion before the detail. Never use 'Two asks. First... Second...' as an opener; a 'Workstreams' heading followed by 'Next Steps'.
docs.engagement_position: This document is dated about 0% of the way through the engagement (at or before kickoff); it runs roughly 7 months end to end. Do not describe progress inconsistent with that position, and do not state the engagement's start or end date.

docs.guidance: Internal memo to the engagement team. Open with a short paragraph on objectives, then a list of workstreams, then next steps. Your personal writing style, held consistently: a formal register (careful qualifications and a measured, precise tone) in short sentences. You habitually: prefers numbered lists over bullet points; sets context in a sentence before making any request. Never use 'Two asks. First... Second...' as an opener; a 'Workstreams' heading followed by 'Next Steps'.
docs.engagement_position: This document is dated about 2% of the way through the engagement (in the early phase); it runs roughly 7 months end to end. Do not describe progress inconsistent with that position, and do not state the engagement's start or end date.

docs.guidance: Minutes of a working session: a list block naming every attendee (full names), discussion summary paragraphs, and a table or list of action items with owners. Your personal writing style, held consistently: a structured register (ordered, enumerative prose with minimal figuration) in short sentences. You habitually: closes with one clear next step; sets context in a sentence before making any request. Never use closing epigrams or aphorisms; the 'rather X now than Y later' antithesis.
docs.engagement_position: This document is dated about 40% of the way through the engagement (around the midpoint); it runs roughly 7 months end to end. Do not describe progress inconsistent with that position, and do not state the engagement's start or end date.
